label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to analyze this ethical dilemma about John, the lawyer who has a choice between attending his son's school play or working on his high-profile case. Let me start by breaking down the problem. First, the main conflict is between John's professional responsibilities and his family obligations. On one side, there's his career as a lawyer, where he's known for working late and handling important cases. On the other side, there's his role as a parent, wanting to be present for his son's event. I should consider the ethical principles involved here. Maybe autonomy? John has the autonomy to choose, but there might be pressure from both sides. Then there's beneficence—doing good—for his family and his profession. Non-maleficence—avoiding harm. If he misses the play, he might harm his relationship with his son. If he doesn't prepare well, he might harm his case. Justice? How fair is it to his son, his colleagues, or his clients? Also, the consequences of each choice. If he goes to the play, maybe he's a better father, but his case might suffer, leading to negative outcomes for his client or his reputation. If he works, he could jeopardize his family's bond, perhaps leading to feelings of neglect in his son or resentment from his spouse. I should think about the different perspectives. From the son's perspective, having his father there is important. From the client's perspective, they need John's full attention for their case. Colleagues might expect him to prioritize work, but maybe also understand family needs. The employer or firm might have expectations about dedication. Are there any ethical frameworks that apply here? Utilitarianism would look at the greatest good for the greatest number. If attending the play would make his son and family happier in the long run, but working might lead to more professional success. Deontological ethics—duty-based. John has a duty to his family and to his professional obligations. Virtue ethics—what would a virtuous person do? Balance between family and career, maybe. Possible solutions: Is there a middle ground? Could John attend part of the play and then go back to work? Or delegate some case tasks to colleagues? Maybe he can prepare earlier so that he can attend the event. But the dilemma states that the case needs his attention now, so maybe that's not possible. Also, the son's play is a one-time event that can't be rescheduled. I should also consider the impact on
